response: The business-research problem is to compare a heuristic and an exact method for the same capacitated maintenance-routing model. Both choose technician routes and jobs served, subject to technician capacity, job eligibility, and route continuity, and minimize travel plus missed-service penalties. The heuristic emphasizes rapid construction of feasible routes; the exact method may improve the incumbent objective and, if its bound closes, certify optimality. A valid study should compare time to feasibility, incumbent quality over time, and certification rates on the same feasible set, objective, and relevant instances. Neither method is yet managerially superior. Rapid feasibility has operational value only if routes face a release deadline or planning cadence, while a certificate has value only if it changes approval, escalation, or another action. Those pathways, along with deadline, cadence, and operating scale, are not supplied. It is therefore unsupported to claim that the faster heuristic improves field performance or that exactness is always more valuable.
